context: But Joel chapter number one will be the focus this evening.
point_summary: Joel 1 is introduced as a text where, amid Israel's history of great rejoicing and revival, joy is conspicuously absent—illustrated by the direct quotation of verse 12's phrase "joy is withered away from the sons of men."

context: In fact, we find that in verse number 12, the Bible says this at the latter part, because joy is withered away from the sons of men.
point_summary: Joel 1:12's phrase "joy is withered away from the sons of men" frames the entire sermon's central question: what causes joy to wither, and can it be restored?

context: And you read just a few verses down in verse number 16, notice what it says, is not the meat cut off before our eyes? Yea, joy and gladness from the house is withered away.
point_summary: Joel 1:16 is cited alongside verse 12 to establish that joy and gladness had visibly withered from God's house, framing the sermon's central question of what causes joy to wither and whether it can be restored.

context: joy is withered away there from verse number 12
point_summary: The withering of joy in Joel 1:12 serves as the sermon's title text, framing the entire message around identifying why joy departs and whether it can be restored, particularly for the backslidden believer.

context: and the fig tree languisheth, the pomegranate tree, the palm tree, also in the apple tree, and even all the trees of the field are withered, because joy is withered away from the sons of men.'" I want to consider as we think about this description that is given to us and we note the situation that is explained in these verses of Scripture, I would submit to you this evening that no doubt this situation is the direct result of sin. It's the result of sin. Hold your place here in Joel because we'll be back here in just a few minutes
point_summary: The total agricultural collapse described in Joel 1—crops, vines, and trees all withered—is presented as the direct result of sin, with Leviticus 26 brought in to establish that covenant disobedience produces exactly this kind of devastation.

context: understand from Joel chapter number one as we analyze and understand what's going on here
point_summary: Joel 1's locust devastation is read as a paradigm of divine judgment on sin, grounded in the principle that God's moral governance—righteousness exalting and sin reproaching any nation—transcends Israel's particular covenant context.

context: gird yourselves and lament, ye priests, how ye ministers of the altar, come, lie all night in sackcloth, ye ministers of my God, for the meat offering and the drink offering is withholden from the house of your God
point_summary: Amid utter destruction, Joel 1:13 presents the solution: priests are called to lament and lie in sackcloth, signifying the repentance and return to God that judgment demands.

context: gird yourselves and lament, ye priests, how ye ministers of the altar, come, lie all night in sackcloth, ye ministers of my God, for the meat offering and the drink offering is withholden from the house of your God
point_summary: Joel 1:14's call to priestly lamentation in sackcloth is presented as the God-given solution to national devastation — repentance, confession, and returning to God amid utter destruction.

context: sanctify ye a fast, call a solemn assembly
point_summary: In response to national devastation, Joel 1:14 prescribes corporate repentance—fasting, solemn assembly, and crying out to God—as the solution that must begin at the house of God.

context: Verse 16 of Joel 1, joy and gladness were missing.
point_summary: The absence of joy and gladness in Joel 1:16 is set against Psalm 51:8's plea to "hear joy and gladness," framing both texts as calls for repentance to restore what sin has taken.

context: The same reason that the nation in Joel 1 was dealing with that utter destruction, sin.
point_summary: Sin is identified as the reason both David in Psalm 51 and the nation in Joel 1 lost their joy, with Joel 1 serving as a parallel case of joy withheld because of sin.

context: I will restore to you the years that the locust hath eaten, the canker worm, the caterpillar, and the palmer worm, my great army which I sent among you.
point_summary: Joel 2:25's promise that God will restore the years the locusts have eaten is applied personally to the believer who has lost joy, grounding that restoration in the character of God as a restoring God.

context: the palmer worm, the locust, the canker worm, and the caterpillar
point_summary: The four waves of destruction named in Joel 1:4 are the very losses God promises in Joel 2:25 to restore, establishing that the same God who sent the devastation is the God who restores.
